message:
fix(cli+server): explain why git_binary_doctor drops reader-thread handles; saturating_add in parse_date_to_micros_end; guard empty-slice panic in median_micros

Three small hardening fixes across three crates. Each has its own
rationale; grouped here because they're all small single-surface
changes and all landed together during the same review pass.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs — git_binary_doctor_entry
- Two call sites in the doctor probe were "silently" dropping the
  stdout/stderr reader thread JoinHandles with `let _ = handle.map(|h|
  h.join())`, which actually *did* block on join. Problem: after
  `child.kill()` + `child.wait()`, if `kill()` failed (for instance
  because a grandchild inherited the pipe and the direct child has
  already exited), the reader threads are still alive waiting on the
  pipe, and `join()` blocks the doctor probe indefinitely. We already
  have the child reaping + return value in hand; the reader threads
  are no longer needed.
- Replace the two `join()` attempts with a short comment explaining
  the intentional drop:
  "We intentionally drop the reader thread handles without joining
   them. If `kill()` failed and the child is still running (e.g. a
   grandchild inherited the pipe), `join()` would hang forever."
  Behaviour: the doctor returns its verdict JSON immediately on
  timeout/failure instead of potentially hanging the whole doctor
  run. The reader threads die naturally when the child is fully
  reaped or when the process exits, whichever comes first.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/mail_ui.rs — parse_date_to_micros_end
- `parse_date_to_micros_end("YYYY-MM-DD")` returns the end-of-day
  microseconds by adding `86_400_000_000 - 1` to the parsed midnight
  timestamp. For dates close to `i64::MAX` (not realistic for real
  calendar dates, but possible with adversarial input), the raw `+`
  overflow would panic in debug builds and wrap silently in release.
- Switch to `ts.saturating_add(86_400_000_000 - 1)` so overflow
  clamps at `i64::MAX` instead of panicking or wrapping. Semantics
  match: any date that saturates was already past any date we'd
  filter against, so clamping to `i64::MAX` still answers the
  intended "end of that day" question correctly.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/tui_poller.rs — median_micros
- `median_micros(values: &mut [u64])` called `values.sort_unstable()`
  unconditionally and then indexed into `values[mid]`. On an empty
  slice that indexes past the end and panics. Call sites could hit
  empty slices when no samples had been collected in the poll
  window.
- Early-return 0 when `values.is_empty()`. Matches how other mean/
  median helpers in this file treat "no samples" (return 0, not
  None / not panic).
